# [Timeline of "foundational" advances in homotopy theory?](/questions/424853/timeline-of-foundational-advances-in-homotopy-theory)

As an interested outsider, I have been intrigued by the number of times that homotopy theory seems to have revamped its foundations over the past fifty years or so. Sometimes there seems to have been a narrowing of focus, via a choice to ignore certain "pathological"—or at least intractably complicated—phenomena; instead of considering all topological spaces, one focuses only on compactly generated spaces or CW complexes or something. Or maybe one chooses to focus only on *stable* homotopy groups. Other times, there seems to have been a broadening of perspective, as new objects of study are introduced to fill perceived gaps in the landscape. Spectra are one notable example. I was fascinated when I discovered the 1991 paper by Lewis, [Is there a convenient category of spectra?](https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-4049(91)90030-6), showing that a certain list of seemingly desirable properties cannot be simultaneously satisfied. More recent concepts include model categories, $\infty$-categories, and homotopy type theory.

I was wondering if someone could sketch a timeline of the most important such "foundational shifts" in homotopy theory over the past 50 years, together with a couple of brief sentences about what motivated the shifts. Such a bird's-eye sketch would, I think, help mathematicians from neighboring fields get some sense of the purpose of all the seemingly high-falutin' modern abstractions, and reduce the impenetrability of the current literature.

## 3 Answers 3

Such a timeline is necessarily highly subjective.

With this disclaimer in mind, we can identify some important turns in the development of foundations of homotopy theory.
The list below concentrates on developments that in some way affect the foundations of homotopy theory, as opposed to general advances in homotopy theory.
Given the length of the list, I probably omitted many important developments,
feel free to point them out in the comments!
I also excluded from consideration the last decade or so, restricting to older developments.

**Poincaré** defined *homology* (via Betti numbers) and the *fundamental group* in a series of papers starting from 1895.
The initial approach was nonrigorous, but in response to the resulting criticism,
Poincaré reformulated his work in terms of simplicial complexes.

**Fréchet** defined *metric spaces* in 1906 and **Hausdorff** defined *topological spaces* in 1914.
This enabled to study the topological properties of spaces without first triangulating them.

Around 1925, Emmy **Noether** proposed to upgrade Betti numbers to *homology groups*.
In connection with this, sometime in 1930s, the terminology shifted from “combinatorial topology” to “algebraic topology”.

Around 1931, **Veblen** and J. H. C. **Whitehead** introduced the modern definition of a *smooth manifold*.

**Eilenberg** defined *singular homology* in 1943,
which resulted in a systematic study of homology and cohomology (defined by **Kolmogoroff** and **Alexander** in 1936)
of arbitrary topological spaces.

Around 1945, **Leray** introduced *sheaves* and *spectral sequences*. The relevant theory was further developed by **Cartan**, **Serre**, and others.

**Eilenberg** and **MacLane** introduced *categories*, *functors*, and *natural transformations* in 1945.
Ever since then, category theory played an increasingly important role in homotopy theory,
to the point where we are now often unable to cleanly separate them.

**Eilenberg** and **Zilber** developed the theory of *simplicial sets* (known at the time as “complete semi-simplicial complexes\*\*) in 1949.

J. H. C. **Whitehead** proved what is now known as the *Whitehead theorem* in 1948.

**Eilenberg** and **Steenrod** published their *Foundations of Algebraic Topology* in 1952,
formulating what is now known as the *Eilenberg–Steenrod axioms*.

Around 1953, **Cartan** and **Eilenberg** completed their book on homological algebra (published in 1956).

**Kan** (advised by **Eilenberg**) systematically developed simplicial homotopy theory (and briefly also cubical homotopy theory)
starting from around 1955.
He introduced combinatorial homotopy groups, the **Dold**–Kan correspondence,
adjoint functors, limits and colimits, Kan extensions, etc.

**Lima** defined *spectra* in 1958.

**Quillen** published his *Homotopical Algebra* in 1967, introducing *model categories*
and using them in his *Rational homotopy theory* around 1968.
Around 1972, he introduced *higher algebraic K-theory*.

In 1971, **Gabriel** and **Ulmer** published their systematic account of *locally presentable categories*.

**Segal** introduced [*Γ-spaces*](https://doi.org/10.1016/0040-9383(74)90022-6) around 1972.
At the same time, **May** introduced operads, also in connection with infinite loop spaces.

**Brown** studied the homotopy theory of [*sheaves of spaces and spectra*](https://doi.org/10.2307/1996573) in 1972.

**Boardman** and **Vogt** introduced [*quasicategories*](https://doi.org/10.1007/BFb0068547) in 1973.

In 1977, **Sullivan** published his work on *rational homotopy theory* in the language of commutative differential graded algebras, complementing the previous work by Quillen.

**Dwyer** and **Kan** introduced and developed the theory of [*simplicial localizations*](https://doi.org/10.1016/0022-4049(80)90049-3) starting from around 1979.

Around 1979, **Bousfield** introduced what is now known as [*Bousfield localizations*](https://doi.org/10.1016/0040-9383(79)90018-1).

In 1983, **Grothendieck** introduced what is now known as *Grothendieck homotopy theory*, as well as *derivators*.

In 1980s, **Joyal** established what is now known as the [*Joyal model structure*](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/model+structure+on+simplicial+sets#joyal_model_structure_on_simplicial_sets) on simplicial sets.

In mid-1980s, **Segal** (following Witten) introduced what is now known as [*functorial field theory*](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-94-015-7809-7_9),
later studied by Atiyah, Kontsevich, Freed, Lawrence, and many others.

In 1985, **Jardine** gave an account of [*simplicial presheaves*](http://math.uchicago.edu/~amathew/simplicialpresheaves.pdf).

Around 1986, **Lewis**, **May**, **Steinberger**, **McClure** introduced genuine [equivariant spectra](https://doi.org/10.1007/BFb0075778).

In 1989, **Makkai** and **Paré** published a systematic account of *accessible categories*.

In 1995, **Baez** and **Dolan** formulated the [*cobordism and tangle hypotheses*](https://arxiv.org/abs/q-alg/9503002), which perhaps qualifies as the first noticeable conjecture
about (∞,n)-categories for arbitrary n.

In 1997, **Elmendorf**, **Kriz**, **Mandell**, **May** published the first ever account of a [*symmetric monoidal category of spectra*](https://web.archive.org/web/20161019233205id_/http://www.math.uchicago.edu:80/~may/BOOKS/EKMM.pdf).

In 1998, **Hovey**, **Shipley**, **Smith** published an account of [*symmetric spectra*](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/9801077).

In 1998, **Rezk** introduced [*complete Segal spaces*](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/9811037).

In the late 1990s, **Voevodsky** introduced and developed *motivic homotopy theory* (including some joint work with **Morel**).

Around the late 1990s, **Smith** introduced *combinatorial model categories*
and proved what is now known as the [*Smith recognition theorem*](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/combinatorial+model+category#SmithTheorem)
and established the existence of left Bousfield localizations of left proper combinatorial model categories.

[*Monoidal model categories*](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0209342) were systematically studied by **Schwede** and **Shipley** starting from 1997.

In 2006 (based on a 2003 preprint), **Lurie**'s [*Higher Topos Theory*](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0608040) came out, first as an online draft, which was later published.

I think Dmitri Pavlov does a nice job laying out a timeline. Instead of writing a competing answer, let me just try to add in a couple of things I think he left out, addressing a few points from the OP.

1940s **Leray** invents [spectral sequences](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectral_sequence) (while imprisoned by the Nazis!) This becomes a critical computational tool in homotopy theory.

1948-1949 **Whitehead** introduces [CW complexes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CW_complex) (and then proves Whitehead's Theorem like Dmitri wrote)

1950 **Serre** introduces [spectral sequences to homotopy theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Serre_spectral_sequence), and they quickly become one of our strongest computational tools.

1962 **Brown** proves what is now known as the [Brown representability theorem](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Brown+representability+theorem). **Adams** proves another important version in 1971. This work allows one to represent a functor $F$ by an object $E$, meaning $F(X) \simeq Hom(X,E)$. There are various versions depending on how you interpret $\simeq$ and $Hom$. Later, this work lets homotopy theorists see the connection between spectra (objects) and generalized cohomology theories (functors).

1967 **Steenrod** gives a list of conditions any category of spaces should satisfy to be called a [convenient category of spaces](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/convenient+category+of+topological+spaces). For one thing, they must be Cartesian closed. The category of [compactly generated spaces](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/compactly+generated+topological+space) is convenient, as is the category of [compactly generated weak Hausdorff spaces](https://stanford.edu/%7Edkim04/posts/the-CGWH-category/) introduced by McCord in 1969 because it has even better properties. The category of CW spaces is "too small", and that's part of the reason simplicial sets gained popularity.

1969-1970: **May** introduces [operads](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Operad) (after closely related concepts used by **Boardman, Vogt, and Kelly**) and uses them for infinite loop space machines. Homotopy theory expands to touch universal algebra.

1983: **Grothendieck** introduces the [homotopy hypothesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_hypothesis) as part of [Pursuing Stacks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pursuing_Stacks), and this guides the deepening connection between homotopy theory and (higher) category theory.

1984 **Ravenel** formulates [the Ravenel conjectures](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ravenel%27s_conjectures) to guide chromatic homotopy theory. This field develops computational tools (to compute stable homotopy groups and other generalized cohomology groups) as well as abstract advances like the [Landweber Exact Functor Theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Landweber_exact_functor_theorem).

1988 **Devinatz, Hopkins, and Smith** [prove the Ravenel conjectures](https://www.jstor.org/stable/1971440), except for the Telescope Conjecture, which was [disproven in 2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.17459). Homotopy theory begins to grow more categorical and more abstract approaches gain traction.

1990: **Goodwillie** introduces [functor calculus](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Goodwillie+calculus), spawning a new branch of homotopy theory. This gives another powerful computational tool.

1990s: I would add **Hovey** to the list of people who systematically studied monoidal model categories. ~~He introduced them, for example~~ His book and his preprint [monoidal model categories](https://arxiv.org/abs/math/9803002) worked out important aspects of the theory. Hovey also introduced [abelian model categories](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/abelian+model+category), by which homotopy theory can touch homological algebra (ok, Quillen did this too) and representation theory (via the stable module category).

1994: Adamek and Rosicky work out the theory of [locally presentable categories](https://books.google.cz/books/about/Locally_Presentable_and_Accessible_Categ.html?id=iXh6rOd7of0C&redir_esc=y), which is foundational to combinatorial model categories and presentable $\infty$-categories, and makes possible many of the advances in Lurie's books in the 2000s.

1998: **Voevodsky** introduces [motivic homotopy theory](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/motivic+homotopy+theory), and uses it to prove the Milnor conjecture. He wins a Fields Medal in 2002. Homotopy theory expands to touch algebraic geometry.

2001: **Mandell, May, Schwede, and Shipley** prove that [the various models of spectra are monoidally equivalent](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/proceedings-of-the-london-mathematical-society/article/abs/model-categories-of-diagram-spectra/B8E98B442D6A4B1E647AAA177E3E1514) in [a strong way](https://www.math.uni-bonn.de/people/schwede/rigid.pdf), hence you can use any of them that you want to.

2005 or so: **Jeff Smith** introduces the category of [Delta-generated spaces](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Delta-generated+topological+space), but never publishes anything about it. Others prove it's convenient and has a combinatorial model structure. All choices of categories of spaces mentioned are equivalent homotopically (I mean Quillen equivalent).

2000s: many different models are introduced for the notion of an [$(\infty,1)$-category](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/(infinity,1)-category), then proven to be equivalent. Same story subsequently for $(\infty,n)$-categories. **Lurie** and others use them to resolve the Cobordism Hypothesis and the Baez-Dolan Stabilization Hypothesis.

2009: **Voevodsky, Awodey, Warren, Shulman**, and others introduce [homotopy type theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_type_theory). Homotopy theory expands to touch type theory, logic, and computer science. Part of the motivation is to improve the foundations of all mathematics and to develop workable proof checking software.

2009: Equivariant homotopy theory experiences a renaissance, thanks to its use by **Hill, Hopkins, and Ravenel** in mostly resolving the Kervaire Invariant One Problem. Multiplicative norms (and, now, $N\_\infty$-operads) and the slice spectral sequence were key advances. Subsequently, advances in spectral sequence techniques allow for both equivariant and motivic homotopy theory to make major advances in our understanding of the stable homotopy groups of spheres (see work of **Dan Isaksen** and co-authors).

2010s: **Lack, Verity, Riehl, Garner, Bourke**, and many others develop homotopy theory for 2-categories and enriched categories, as well as the $\infty$-cosmoi approach to $\infty$-category theory.

2022-2023: Thanks to major advances in algebraic $K$-theory, trace methods, THH, and TC, the Chromatic Redshift Conjecture is proven and the Telescope Conjecture is disproven.

I do not intend to edit this in perpetuity. Future readers should note when the question was asked and when the answers were posted, and take this thread as a snapshot.

Thanks to both Pavlov and White there is now an almost complete list of "critical points" in the history of homotopy theory.

There are a few items that perhaps should make it into the list, for instance the Archaic Period: Betti introduced Betti's numbers [here](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/BF02420029) in 1870 -incidentally, as simple as they are, Betti's numbers continue to play a dramatic role in applied math, for instance in Persistent Homology.

I would suggest one tiny emendation: extend your 50 years slightly, by five years, to include a true **Annus Mirabilis** (1967), namely the work of Quillen on **Model Categories**.

Let me say why I feel this is indeed a breakthrough and the beginning of modern era (some of the work after 2005 is properly speaking not modern, but post-modern, see below).

Before Quillen, in 1952 Steenrod and Eilenberg managed to get the **Grand Unification of Cohomology**, certainly a major breakthrough in the series of "foundational efforts" in Algebraic Topology.

In a way Quillen tried to do the same for homotopical algebra, by introducing a sets of axioms for "doing homotopy " in a category. The key notion here is **weak equivalence**, ie a sets of maps in the ambient cat which contain all the isomorphisms.

This simple step is a foundational paradigm change, because it tells us WHAT Homotopy is all about:

**we move from equality (set theory)\_ to isomorphism (category theory) to equivalence (homotopy theory**).

Quillen add some axioms on formal fibrations, cofibrations, to compute the so-called homotopy limits and colimits, ie lims and colims "up to homotopy"

NOTE before quillen folks knew about hom lim and hom colims: start with a cat with a model structure, and "localize" it, ie formally invert all the weak equivalences. The new cat, called the homotopy category, is is general not well behaved as far as standard lims and colims: one has thus to introduce a new kind of universal objects appropriate for the homotopic context.

So, from the Annus Mirabilis begins a new chapter, but it does not end there. As a result of Quillen's shift, now many many "things" that were not under the rubric of homotopy theory, structures that are not even topological, acquire an homotopic flavor.

Funny enough, one of these is category theory itself: Cat, the category of small cats, has a default model structure, where weak equivalences are simply cats equivalence.

There were several attempts to generalize and expand on the look at Homotopy given by model structures, but if we focus on true radically new insights, here it goes:

back in the golden era, a standard homotopy was simply a continuous deformation, so essentially an invertible path between maps. In Quillen you start with the weak equivalences, but let us go back to the continuous deformation: if the cat where I want to introduce my weak equivalences happens to be a 2-cat, and I look at the groupoid of 2-maps therein, I have my continuous deformations.

So, here is the key insight that migrates from the modern approach to the post-modern: do not look at a single cat alone, but see it as as only the ground floor of a higher and higher groupoid (paths, paths of paths, etc.) Rather than being the bedrock of Homotopy, model structures become models, or presentations, of the REAL OBJECT of Homotopy, the **invariant infinity groupoid** in all its splendor. That basic insight is already in Grothendieck, around 1983, maybe earlier, but has blossomed into an entire field thanks to Voevodsky, Lurie, Rezk, etc.

What is fascinating, is that the post-modern era is not simply foundational, but admits also a foundationalist approach: a large swath of mathematics in principle can be seen from this angle, of "getting rid of equality", and replacing it with equivalences and their higher versions. .

FULL CONTENT:
# nLab homotopy theory FAQ

This page is to provide non-technical or maybe semi-technical discussion of the nature and role of [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory). For more technical details and further pointers see at [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory).

# Contents

## What is homotopy theory?

## Is homotopy theory a part of algebraic topology?

On the relation between [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) and ([algebraic](/nlab/show/algebraic+topology)) [topology](/nlab/show/topology):

In one of the first textbooks on [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory), *[An introduction to homotopy theory](homotopy+theory#Hilton53)* by [Peter J. Hilton](/nlab/show/Peter+J.+Hilton) (1953), one reads:

Since the introduction of [homotopy groups](/nlab/show/homotopy+groups) by [Hurewicz](/nlab/show/Hurewicz) in 1935, [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) has been occupying an increasingly prominent place in the field of [algebraic topology](/nlab/show/algebraic+topology). Important new advances are continually being made in the subject by various workers; and the recent developments emanating from the French school of topologists underline the desirability of having available a basic introduction to [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) suitable for those who wish to undertake research in the subject and for those who wish to be in a position to understand the modern techniques and results.

[Sze-Tsen Hu](/nlab/show/Sze-Tsen+Hu) writes in *[Homotopy Theory](homotopy+theory#SzeTsen59)* (1959):

The recognition of the branch of mathematics now called [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) took place in the few years after the introduction of [homotopy groups](/nlab/show/homotopy+groups) by [Witold Hurewicz](/nlab/show/Witold+Hurewicz) in 1935. Since then, with numerous advances made by various workers, it has been playing an increasingly important role in the expanding field of [algebraic topology](/nlab/show/algebraic+topology). However, there exists no textbook on the subject at any level except the extremely condensed Cambridge tract of [P. J. Hilton](/nlab/show/P.+J.+Hilton) entitled “[An Introduction to Homotopy Theory](homotopy+theory#Hilton53)”.

Thus, at the time [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) was clearly identified as a part of [algebraic topology](/nlab/show/algebraic+topology).

The 1995 *[Handbook of Algebraic Topology](/nlab/show/Handbook+of+Algebraic+Topology)* (edited by [Ioan M. James](/nlab/show/Ioan+M.+James)) is overwhelmingly devoted to [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory).

However, the attitudes have changed since then. [Haynes Miller](/nlab/show/Haynes+Miller) writes in the *[Handbook of Homotopy Theory](/nlab/show/Handbook+of+Homotopy+Theory)* (2019):

This volume may be regarded as a successor to the “[Handbook of Algebraic Topology](/nlab/show/Handbook+of+Algebraic+Topology),” edited by [Ioan James](/nlab/show/Ioan+James) and published a quarter of a century ago. In calling it the “[Handbook of Homotopy Theory](/nlab/show/Handbook+of+Homotopy+Theory),” I am recognizing that the discipline has expanded and deepened, and traditional questions of [topology](/nlab/show/topology), as classically understood, are now only one of many distinct mathematical disciplines in which it has had a profound impact and which serve as sources of motivation for research directions within [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) proper.

[Clark Barwick](/nlab/show/Clark+Barwick) writes in *[The future of homotopy theory](https://www.maths.ed.ac.uk/~cbarwick/papers/future.pdf)* (2018):

Neither our subject nor its interaction with other areas of inquiry is widely understood. Some of us [4{}^4](#BarwickFootnote4) call ourselves *algebraic topologists*, but this has the unhelpful effect of making the subject appear to be an area of [topology](/nlab/show/topology), which I think is profoundly inaccurate. It so happens that one way (and historically the first way) to model homotopical thinking is to employ a very particular class of [topological spaces](/nlab/show/topological+spaces). [5{}^5](#BarwickFootnote5) Today, the praxis of [homotopy](/nlab/show/homotopy)theory interacts with [topology](/nlab/show/topology) no more often than it does with [arithmetic geometry](/nlab/show/arithmetic+geometry) and [category theory](/nlab/show/category+theory), and the interactions with areas like [representation theory](/nlab/show/representation+theory) are growing rapidly. *Homotopy theory is not a branch of topology.* This is important, because as long as homotopy theory is classified under the umbrella of topology, there will be errors of judgement in who is considered competent to judge our work; the results of this at journals, on the job market, and in funding is real and lasting.

4{}^4 not me

5{}^5 I think of homotopy theory as an enrichment of the notion of [equality](/nlab/show/equality), dedicated to the primacy of *[structure over properties](/nlab/show/stuff%2C+structure%2C+property)*. Simplistic and abstract though this idea is, it leads rapidly to a whole universe of nontrivial structures.

## What objects does homotopy theory study?

We list some [mathematical objects](/nlab/show/mathematical+objects) that are undoubtedly studied by [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory):

The first object is known under various names: [∞-groupoid](/nlab/show/%E2%88%9E-groupoid), (homotopy) space, [(homotopy) type](/nlab/show/homotopy+type), more recent (and so far exotic) names include “anima”. See the [section on “spaces”](#spaces) for an explanation of why so many different names are used for (essentially) the same concept. These objects can be modeled by [simplicial sets](/nlab/show/simplicial+sets) up to [simplicial weak equivalence](/nlab/show/simplicial+weak+equivalence), or [topological spaces](/nlab/show/topological+spaces) up to [weak homotopy equivalence](/nlab/show/weak+homotopy+equivalence), or many other models. See the [section on models](#models) for more details about models.

[Spectra](/nlab/show/Spectra) or [stable homotopy types](/nlab/show/stable+homotopy+types).

Specific [models](#models) include [sequential spectra](/nlab/show/sequential+spectra) or [symmetric spectra](/nlab/show/symmetric+spectra) (valued in [simplicial sets](/nlab/show/simplicial+sets) or [topological spaces](/nlab/show/topological+spaces)), [orthogonal spectra](/nlab/show/orthogonal+spectra), [reduced excisive functors](/nlab/show/reduced+excisive+functors), etc.

[∞-categories](/nlab/show/%E2%88%9E-categories), also known as [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories), [homotopy theories](/nlab/show/homotopy+theories), etc.

Specific [models](#models) include [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories), [quasicategories](/nlab/show/quasicategories), [simplicial categories](/nlab/show/simplicial+categories), [Segal categories](/nlab/show/Segal+categories), [complete Segal spaces](/nlab/show/complete+Segal+spaces), etc. See the [section on ∞-categories](#infinity) for more information.

[(∞,n)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2Cn%29-categories), which can be [modeled](#models) by [globular n-fold complete Segal spaces](/nlab/show/globular+n-fold+complete+Segal+spaces), Rezk’s [Theta-n spaces](/nlab/show/Theta-n+spaces), etc.

## What objects does algebraic topology study?

As already explained in the [introduction](#intro), there is a recent tendency to separate [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) from [algebraic topology](/nlab/show/algebraic+topology). This leaves the question as to what exactly [algebraic topology](/nlab/show/algebraic+topology) is once the separation happens.

We list some [mathematical objects](/nlab/show/mathematical+objects) that are undoubtedly studied by [algebraic topology](/nlab/show/algebraic+topology):

[manifolds](/nlab/show/manifolds), as studied in [surgery theory](/nlab/show/surgery+theory), [bordism theory](/nlab/show/bordism+theory), etc.

These include [topological manifolds](/nlab/show/topological+manifolds), [PL-manifolds](/nlab/show/PL-manifolds), and [smooth manifolds](/nlab/show/smooth+manifolds).

[knots](/nlab/show/knots), [tangles](/nlab/show/tangles), [surfaces](/nlab/show/surfaces), and other objects arising from [manifolds](/nlab/show/manifolds).

[Lie groups](/nlab/show/Lie+groups) and [characteristic classes](/nlab/show/characteristic+classes).

[polyhedra](/nlab/show/polyhedra).

## What are “spaces” in homotopy theory?

These are some of the first and most important objects introduced in [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory).

This is witnessed by the many names given to them, which all describe the same concept but have a slightly different emphasis:

Yet even this large variety of viewpoints is sometimes deemed insufficient, which leads mathematicians to introduce further names for such objects, such as “anima” used by [Dustin Clausen](/nlab/show/Dustin+Clausen) and [Peter Scholze](/nlab/show/Peter+Scholze), alluding to the process of [vertical categorification](/nlab/show/vertical+categorification), in its specific incarnation as groupoidal categorification, alias homotopification, which can be seen as “animating” 1-categorical objects by introducing higher homotopies into them.

We emphasize that these objects (except for [types](/nlab/show/types) in [homotopy type theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+type+theory)) come with an associated notion of [weak equivalence](/nlab/show/weak+equivalence), which is *not* isomorphism. For example, when using [topological spaces](/nlab/show/topological+spaces) as [models](#models) for (homotopy) spaces, we are *not* interested in invariants under [homeomorphisms](/nlab/show/homeomorphisms), but rather only under [weak homotopy equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+homotopy+equivalences). See the [section on models](#models) for more details.

## What is a model in homotopy theory?

Informal ideas of objects studied by [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory), such as (homotopy) spaces or [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories), must be presented using rigorous mathematical definitions. As of April 2020, this most likely means defining them using some flavor of [set theory](/nlab/show/set+theory), although [homotopy type theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+type+theory) may emerge as an alternative in the future.

This process introduces artifacts that are not present in the original informal idea and have no meaning in that context. As an example, suppose we present a (homotopy) space using a [simplicial set](/nlab/show/simplicial+set). Then the [set](/nlab/show/set) of 0-simplices or nn-simplices for any n≥0n\ge0 is a piece of data specific to our chosen presentation. Another presentation may have a set of 0-simplices of different cardinality, say. On the other hand, if we take the set of [connected components](/nlab/show/connected+components) of a [simplicial set](/nlab/show/simplicial+set), the resulting set describes a property of the underlying (homotopy) space and not just some specific presentation. This can be formalized by saying that the set of connected components is invariant under [simplicial weak equivalences](/nlab/show/simplicial+weak+equivalences).

Thus [simplicial sets](/nlab/show/simplicial+sets) up to [simplicial weak equivalences](/nlab/show/simplicial+weak+equivalences) *model* (homotopy) spaces.

One common way to encode models is to organize them in a [relative category](/nlab/show/relative+category): the underlying category encodes the specific presentation, whereas weak equivalences encode the relevant notion of “sameness”.

## Why do we need models in homotopy theory?

The short answer is: we do not know how to work without models.

A good example is the notion of an [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category), which exists at the [Platonic](/nlab/show/Platonic) level, and whose “shadows” (using Plato’s terminology) in the world of rigorous mathematics have been exhibited as various models of [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories): [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories), [quasicategories](/nlab/show/quasicategories), [Segal categories](/nlab/show/Segal+categories), [complete Segal spaces](/nlab/show/complete+Segal+spaces), [simplicial categories](/nlab/show/simplicial+categories), etc.

However, the concept of [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories) per se resists formalization in a satisfactory way. [Homotopy type theory](/nlab/show/Homotopy+type+theory) may succeed one day on this path, but so far no working definition has been exhibited yet.

## What is an ∞-category or (∞,1)-category?

An (∞,1)-category is a category [enriched](/nlab/show/enriched) in (homotopy) spaces.

This can be formalized in many different ways. For example, if we [model](#models) homotopy spaces by [simplicial sets](/nlab/show/simplicial+sets) with [simplicial weak equivalences](/nlab/show/simplicial+weak+equivalences), then we can consider categories enriched in [simplicial sets](/nlab/show/simplicial+sets), equipped with [Dwyer–Kan equivalences](/nlab/show/Dwyer%E2%80%93Kan+equivalences), which are [enriched functors](/nlab/show/enriched+functors) that induce a [simplicial weak equivalence](/nlab/show/simplicial+weak+equivalence) on each mapping [simplicial set](/nlab/show/simplicial+set), and after passing to the [homotopy category](/nlab/show/homotopy+category) (which in this case amounts to replacing each mapping [simplicial set](/nlab/show/simplicial+set) with its set of connected components), we get an [equivalence of categories](/nlab/show/equivalence+of+categories).

As one can see from this description, there are substantial [modeling](#models) issues related to (∞,1)-categories.

Other [models](#models) for (∞,1)-categories include [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories), [quasicategories](/nlab/show/quasicategories), [Segal categories](/nlab/show/Segal+categories), [complete Segal spaces](/nlab/show/complete+Segal+spaces), etc.

All these models are equivalent in a precise sense: we can organize each of them into a [Quillen model category](/nlab/show/Quillen+model+category) so that all resulting [model categories](/nlab/show/model+categories) are [Quillen equivalent](/nlab/show/Quillen+equivalent).

The term “[∞-category](/nlab/show/%E2%88%9E-category)” has two different meanings: it can either mean (∞,1)-category, or (more recently) it can mean a [quasicategory](/nlab/show/quasicategory) (as used in [Lurie](/nlab/show/Lurie)‘s books, for example).

## Are quasicategories model-independent?

No, for example, the cardinality of the set of 0-simplices of a small [quasicategory](/nlab/show/quasicategory) does not have any model-independent meaning, although it is an [isomorphism](/nlab/show/isomorphism) invariant.

Unfortunately, one often sees the adjective “model-independent” (ab)used to mean that only constructions that are meaningful on the level of underlying [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories) are used in a particular argument with [quasicategories](/nlab/show/quasicategories). Of course, there is nothing special about [quasicategories](/nlab/show/quasicategories) in this context: we often write “model-independent” arguments of such type also using [model categories](/nlab/show/model+categories), for example.

## What is homotopy type theory?

See [homotopy type theory FAQ](/nlab/show/homotopy+type+theory+FAQ) for a detailed explanation. Here we only offer a highly impressionistic description.

Consider the traditional mathematical setup for (homotopy) spaces [modeled](#models) by [simplicial sets](/nlab/show/simplicial+sets) and [simplicial weak equivalences](/nlab/show/simplicial+weak+equivalences): we start with [first-order logic](/nlab/show/first-order+logic), introduce [Zermelo-Fraenkel axioms](/nlab/show/Zermelo-Fraenkel+axioms), then define [simplicial sets](/nlab/show/simplicial+sets) and [simplicial weak equivalences](/nlab/show/simplicial+weak+equivalences).

[Homotopy type theory](/nlab/show/Homotopy+type+theory) replaces this entire stack with a flavor of [type theory](/nlab/show/type+theory) that aims to axiomatize homotopy types (known in the field simply as types) directly, without these intermediate steps.

In particular, [first-order logic](/nlab/show/first-order+logic) is subsumed into [type theory](/nlab/show/type+theory). The latter (very) roughly resembles [ETCS](/nlab/show/ETCS) in how it chooses to formulate things like existence of (co)products of sets etc., i.e., these concepts are formulated in a categorical way rather than through constructions like [Kuratowski pairs](/nlab/show/Kuratowski+pairs) etc.

More fundamentally, the meaning of equality sign == is altered: now A=BA=B (very) roughly means the the space of paths from the point AA to the point BB in whatever ambient space contains the points AA and BB. Very roughly, if this space is empty, we could interpret this as A≠BA\ne B and if it is nonempty, we could interpret this as A=BA=B. However, [homotopy type theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+type+theory) preserves the whole homotopy type of the path space and not just whether it is empty or not. The (Platonic) meaning of the equality sign is thus fundamentally enhanced and [homotopy type theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+type+theory) explains how to manipulate such enhanced equalities.

This setup allows us to talk about homotopy types directly, without using models. Furthermore, the analog of weak equivalences in this setting behaves similar to [isomorphisms](/nlab/show/isomorphisms) (with a caveat that [equality](/nlab/show/equality) is now understood in the enhanced sense). In particular, every equivalence is invertible, unlike (say) [simplicial weak equivalences](/nlab/show/simplicial+weak+equivalences), which need not be.

Much has been achieved on this path, including a new proof of the [Blakers-Massey theorem](/nlab/show/Blakers-Massey+theorem) that led to new classical consequences after being instantiated in [(∞,1)-toposes](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-toposes).

However, one serious obstacle to adopting this approach in mainstream [homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) is that there is currently (April 2020) no working definition of an [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category), although research is being conducted in this direction.

## What is homotopical algebra?

[Homotopical Algebra](/nlab/show/Homotopical+Algebra) is the title of a 1967 book by [Daniel Quillen](/nlab/show/Daniel+Quillen) that introduced [model categories](/nlab/show/model+categories).

Ever since then this term has been used to describe research in the area of [model categories](/nlab/show/model+categories).

It is often hard to separate homotopical algebra from homotopy theory. Typically, “homotopical algebra” is used to refer to arguments that use notions from [model categories](/nlab/show/model+categories) rather explicitly, as opposed to (say) [quasicategorical](/nlab/show/quasicategorical) arguments. We do note that setting up the theory of [quasicategories](/nlab/show/quasicategories) relies heavily on [homotopical algebra](/nlab/show/homotopical+algebra) and much of Lurie’s [Higher Topos Theory](/nlab/show/Higher+Topos+Theory) is devoted to rather subtle aspects of [homotopical algebra](/nlab/show/homotopical+algebra).

## How are model categories related to other models of ∞-categories?

If we discard [cofibrations](/nlab/show/cofibrations) and [fibrations](/nlab/show/fibrations) from the data of a [model category](/nlab/show/model+category), we get a [relative category](/nlab/show/relative+category). [Relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+category) are one of many equivalent [models](#models) for [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories), so any [model category](/nlab/show/model+category) has an underlying [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category).

Since the underlying [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) only depends on [weak equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+equivalences) and not on [cofibrations](/nlab/show/cofibrations) or [fibrations](/nlab/show/fibrations), we deduce that two [model categories](/nlab/show/model+categories) with the same [weak equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+equivalences), but different [cofibrations](/nlab/show/cofibrations) or [fibrations](/nlab/show/fibrations) have the same underlying [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category).

Thus, the data of [cofibrations](/nlab/show/cofibrations) and [fibrations](/nlab/show/fibrations) merely enhances the given presentation of an [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) as a [relative category](/nlab/show/relative+category) with additional data that helps to organize various computations, e.g., to [derive functors](#derived).

However, the ultimate answer to any computation with [model categories](/nlab/show/model+categories) that makes sense on the level of underlying [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories) does not depend on the choice of [cofibrations](/nlab/show/cofibrations) and [fibrations](/nlab/show/fibrations), although its specific [model](#models) certainly does.

Another important aspect of the relation between [model categories](/nlab/show/model+categories) and [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories) is that a [model category](/nlab/show/model+category) must satisfy additional (co)completeness conditions. In the original definition by [Quillen](/nlab/show/Quillen), a [model category](/nlab/show/model+category) must be finitely (co)complete, and its (co)fibrations need not be closed under [retracts](/nlab/show/retracts). In this case, the underlying [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) admits finite ∞-(co)limits. In the revised definition by [Kan](/nlab/show/Kan), a [model category](/nlab/show/model+category) must be (co)complete, and its (co)fibrations must be closed under [retracts](/nlab/show/retracts). In this case, the underlying [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) admits small ∞-(co)limits. Finally, Hovey’s definition additionally requires factorizations to be functorial. This condition does not seem to alter the class of underlying [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories).

## What is the relationship between (higher) category theory and homotopy theory?

[Homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) serves as a foundation for much of modern (higher) [category theory](/nlab/show/category+theory). For instance, the proof of equivalence of various models of [(∞,n)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2Cn%29-categories) heavily relies on [homotopical algebra](#halg). It is difficult to draw a sharp boundary between the two subjects; [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories) could definitely be seen as being part of both, for example.

[Homotopy theory](/nlab/show/homotopy+theory) also somewhat implicitly permeates classical [category theory](/nlab/show/category+theory). For example, the correct notion of a [pullback](/nlab/show/pullback) for diagrams of [categories](/nlab/show/categories) is not the strict [pullback](/nlab/show/pullback), but rather incorporates an additional isomorphism between the images of two given objects.

But this is precisely the [homotopy pullback](/nlab/show/homotopy+pullback) in the category of [small categories](/nlab/show/small+categories) equipped with the [natural model structure](/nlab/show/natural+model+structure).

## What is a derived functor?

Just as [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories) [model](#models) [(∞,1)-categories](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-categories), we can expect functors between [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories) to [model](#models) [(∞,1)-functors](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-functors).

The easiest way to perform such [modeling](#models) is to require functors to preserve [weak equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+equivalences). This indeed works perfectly well. However, many functors arising in practice, such as the tensor product of [chain complexes](/nlab/show/chain+complexes) (with [quasi-isomorphisms](/nlab/show/quasi-isomorphisms) as [weak equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+equivalences)) does not satisfy such a strong condition.

“Deriving” is a process that [models](#models) [(∞,1)-functors](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-functors) via functors between [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories) that need not preserve [weak equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+equivalences).

Multiple nonequivalent definitions of [derived functors](/nlab/show/derived+functors) exist in the literature, and functors that can be derived in one of the definitions need not be derivable in another.

Typically (in most definitions), deriving a [functor](/nlab/show/functor) F:C→DF\colon C\to D between [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories) CC and DD involves replacing FF with F∘RF\circ R, where R:C→CR\colon C\to C is a [resolution](/nlab/show/resolution) functor, which is typically require to preserve [weak equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+equivalences) and be itself a [weak equivalence](/nlab/show/weak+equivalence) of [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories) (known as a [Dwyer-Kan equivalence](/nlab/show/Dwyer-Kan+equivalence) of [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories)).

A definition with rather good properties was given recently by [Hinich](/nlab/show/Hinich) and relies on converting a given functor F:C→DF\colon C\to D into a [cocartesian fibration](/nlab/show/cocartesian+fibration) (or [cartesian fibration](/nlab/show/cartesian+fibration)) over the category {0→1}\{0\to1\}, and equipping the total category of this fibration with an obvious notion of weak equivalence. If the resulting functor of relative categories is a (co)cartesian fibration of relative categories, we can convert it to a functor C→DC\to D that preserves [weak equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+equivalences), which is the (left or right) [derived functor](/nlab/show/derived+functor) of FF.

Other (older) definitions involve [Kan extensions](/nlab/show/Kan+extensions) along [localization](/nlab/show/localization) functors to [homotopy categories](/nlab/show/homotopy+categories). These definitions do not have such nice theoretical properties as the definition considered above. For example, they tend to misbehave when we try to derive compositions of functors.

## What is the homotopy category of an (∞,1)-category? What are its limitations?

The [homotopy category](/nlab/show/homotopy+category) Ho(C)Ho(C) of an [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) CC is obtained by replacing each of the hom [∞-groupoids](/nlab/show/%E2%88%9E-groupoids) hom(x,y)hom(x,y) with its set of connected components: hom Ho(C)(x,y):=π 0(hom C(x,y))hom\_{Ho(C)}(x,y) := \pi\_0(hom\_C(x,y)).

This informal description can be formalized in any [model](#models) of (∞,1)-categories. For instance, for [relative categories](/nlab/show/relative+categories) we can formally invert all [weak equivalences](/nlab/show/weak+equivalences), the resulting [localization](/nlab/show/localization) being the [homotopy category](/nlab/show/homotopy+category). (We note that inverting morphisms *up to homotopy* instead of strictly simply recovers the underlying [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) of a [relative category](/nlab/show/relative+category).)

The [homotopy category](/nlab/show/homotopy+category) is a useful tool for extracting invariants from a given [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category). For example, in the [relative category](/nlab/show/relative+category) of [chain complexes](/nlab/show/chain+complexes) with [quasi-isomorphisms](/nlab/show/quasi-isomorphisms) we can compute

However, passing to the [homotopy category](/nlab/show/homotopy+category) destroys a tremendous amount of information about the underlying [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category). In particular, it is rarely possible to recover any information about [(∞,1)-limits](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-limits) and [(∞,1)-colimits](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-colimits) from the [homotopy category](/nlab/show/homotopy+category), other than the case of [products](/nlab/show/products) and [coproducts](/nlab/show/coproducts). Thus, typically the passage to the [homotopy category](/nlab/show/homotopy+category) happens near the end of an argument, where one no longer needs categorical constructions like limits and colimits.

## What is a triangulated category? What are its limitations?

A [stable (∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/stable+%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) is an [(∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) that admits finite (∞,1)-(co)limits and such that [cartesian squares](/nlab/show/cartesian+squares) coincide with [cocartesian squares](/nlab/show/cocartesian+squares) and there is a morphism from the [terminal object](/nlab/show/terminal+object) to the [initial object](/nlab/show/initial+object).

As is clear from the definition, being a stable (∞,1)-category is a *property* of an (∞,1)-category. Roughly speaking, a *triangulated category* is the [homotopy category](/nlab/show/homotopy+category) of a [stable (∞,1)-category](/nlab/show/stable+%28%E2%88%9E%2C1%29-category) equipped with the additional data of a [homotopy cofiber](/nlab/show/homotopy+cofiber) of any morphism.

The above is not quite true. In reality, one axiomatizes [triangulated categories](/nlab/show/triangulated+categories) as [additive categories](/nlab/show/additive+categories) with additional data of distinguished triangles (basically, [homotopy cofiber](/nlab/show/homotopy+cofiber) sequences) and suspension functor that satisfies a bunch of axioms. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Branch of mathematics

In [mathematics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), **homotopy theory** is a systematic study of situations in which [maps](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Map_(mathematics) "Map (mathematics)") can come with [homotopies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy "Homotopy") between them. It originated as a topic in [algebraic topology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algebraic_topology "Algebraic topology"), but nowadays is learned as an independent discipline.

Applications to other fields of mathematics
-------------------------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Homotopy_theory&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Applications to other fields of mathematics")]

Besides algebraic topology, the theory has also been used in other areas of mathematics such as:

*   [Algebraic geometry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algebraic_geometry "Algebraic geometry") (e.g., [A 1 homotopy theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/A1_homotopy_theory "A1 homotopy theory"))
*   [Category theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category_theory "Category theory") (specifically the study of [higher categories](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Higher_category_theory "Higher category theory"))

Concepts
--------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Homotopy_theory&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Concepts")]

### Spaces and maps

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Homotopy_theory&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Spaces and maps")]

In homotopy theory and algebraic topology, the word "space" denotes a [topological space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Topological_space "Topological space"). In order to avoid [pathologies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pathological_(mathematics) "Pathological (mathematics)"), one rarely works with arbitrary spaces; instead, one requires spaces to meet extra constraints, such as being [compactly generated weak Hausdorff](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Category_of_compactly_generated_weak_Hausdorff_spaces "Category of compactly generated weak Hausdorff spaces") or a [CW complex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CW_complex "CW complex").

In the same vein as above, a "[map](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Map_(mathematics) "Map (mathematics)")" is a continuous function, possibly with some extra constraints.

Often, one works with a [pointed space](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pointed_space "Pointed space")—that is, a space with a "distinguished point", called a basepoint. A pointed map is then a map which preserves basepoints; that is, it sends the basepoint of the domain to that of the codomain. In contrast, a free map is one which needn't preserve basepoints.

The Cartesian product of two pointed spaces X,Y{\displaystyle X,Y}![Image 4: {\displaystyle X,Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b8705438171d938b7f59cd1bfa5b7d99b6afa5cd) are not naturally pointed. A substitute is the [smash product](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Smash_product "Smash product")X∧Y{\displaystyle X\wedge Y}![Image 5: {\displaystyle X\wedge Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6edc6915b42026ef5d46c585f7e44955f2d15ecf) which is characterized by the [adjoint relation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adjoint_functor "Adjoint functor")

Map⁡(X∧Y,Z)=Map⁡(X,Map⁡(Y,Z)){\displaystyle \operatorname {Map} (X\wedge Y,Z)=\operatorname {Map} (X,\operatorname {Map} (Y,Z))}![Image 6: {\displaystyle \operatorname {Map} (X\wedge Y,Z)=\operatorname {Map} (X,\operatorname {Map} (Y,Z))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c7c9565c7ff0768fabf7bb5cb2c57d19b905fa96),
that is, a smash product is an analog of a [tensor product](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensor_product "Tensor product") in abstract algebra (see [tensor-hom adjunction](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensor-hom_adjunction "Tensor-hom adjunction")). Explicitly, X∧Y{\displaystyle X\wedge Y}![Image 7: {\displaystyle X\wedge Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6edc6915b42026ef5d46c585f7e44955f2d15ecf) is the quotient of X×Y{\displaystyle X\times Y}![Image 8: {\displaystyle X\times Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1613c1ff4b6fbfb6c80a8da83e90ad28f0ab3483) by the [wedge sum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wedge_sum "Wedge sum")X∨Y{\displaystyle X\vee Y}![Image 9: {\displaystyle X\vee Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/091f7bb09b74960d59d46cc57a297ae37aece6e8).

### Homotopy

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Homotopy_theory&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Homotopy")]

Main article: [Homotopy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy "Homotopy")

Let _I_ denote the unit interval [0,1]{\displaystyle [0,1]}![Image 10: {\displaystyle [0,1]}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/738f7d23bb2d9642bab520020873cccbef49768d). A map

h:X×I→Y{\displaystyle h:X\times I\to Y}![Image 11: {\displaystyle h:X\times I\to Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1f240a8efc3ead4c041585116d590cdfb0e4df75)
is called a [homotopy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy "Homotopy") from the map h 0{\displaystyle h_{0}}![Image 12: {\displaystyle h_{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/08c1c908b03c3f63383c7199465c7fd0b105030f) to the map h 1{\displaystyle h_{1}}![Image 13: {\displaystyle h_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/14e8880a2e4243a2fe5157e574a0547ef3d5d373), where h t(x)=h(x,t){\displaystyle h_{t}(x)=h(x,t)}![Image 14: {\displaystyle h_{t}(x)=h(x,t)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/04b80f80ea8df70ed6fda9cd793469d578025501). Intuitively, we may think of h{\displaystyle h}![Image 15: {\displaystyle h}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b26be3e694314bc90c3215047e4a2010c6ee184a) as a path from the map h 0{\displaystyle h_{0}}![Image 16: {\displaystyle h_{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/08c1c908b03c3f63383c7199465c7fd0b105030f) to the map h 1{\displaystyle h_{1}}![Image 17: {\displaystyle h_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/14e8880a2e4243a2fe5157e574a0547ef3d5d373). Indeed, a homotopy can be shown to be an [equivalence relation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Equivalence_relation "Equivalence relation"). When _X_, _Y_ are pointed spaces, the maps h t{\displaystyle h_{t}}![Image 18: {\displaystyle h_{t}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e8dbf3d8bfe322f68ff6400385578f8d78e1ba7c) are required to preserve the basepoint and the homotopy h{\displaystyle h}![Image 19: {\displaystyle h}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b26be3e694314bc90c3215047e4a2010c6ee184a) is called a [based homotopy](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Based_homotopy&action=edit&redlink=1 "Based homotopy (page does not exist)"). A based homotopy is the same as a (based) map X∧I+→Y{\displaystyle X\wedge I_{+}\to Y}![Image 20: {\displaystyle X\wedge I_{+}\to Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9a9ae72755c47f18e95a6484b51dc4fd34ee7475) where I+{\displaystyle I_{+}}![Image 21: {\displaystyle I_{+}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/03b29b97c4ce7b7a7551cf9f537a5fa42c14c145) is I{\displaystyle I}![Image 22: {\displaystyle I}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/535ea7fc4134a31cbe2251d9d3511374bc41be9f) together with a disjoint basepoint.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_theory#cite_note-1)

Given a pointed space _X_ and an [integer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Integer "Integer")n≥0{\displaystyle n\geq 0}![Image 23: {\displaystyle n\geq 0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ce8a1b7b3bc3c790054d93629fc3b08cd1da1fd0), let π n X=[S n,X]{\displaystyle \pi _{n}X=[S^{n},X]}![Image 24: {\displaystyle \pi _{n}X=[S^{n},X]}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/01933c254562233f07b4a64a076e6aa6337e066a) be the homotopy classes of based maps S n→X{\displaystyle S^{n}\to X}![Image 25: {\displaystyle S^{n}\to X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/21881e91b66e1c3c1fd8803e144986cb52bec9ad) from a (pointed) _n_-sphere S n{\displaystyle S^{n}}![Image 26: {\displaystyle S^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ee006452a59bf1eb29983b4412348b66517a2d23) to _X_. As it turns out,

*   for n≥1{\displaystyle n\geq 1}![Image 27: {\displaystyle n\geq 1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d8ce9ce38d06f6bf5a3fe063118c09c2b6202bfe), π n X{\displaystyle \pi _{n}X}![Image 28: {\displaystyle \pi _{n}X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/309cf040236632f263afb0b16d69c0f0fa4f2140) are [groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_(mathematics) "Group (mathematics)") called [homotopy groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_group "Homotopy group"); in particular, π 1 X{\displaystyle \pi _{1}X}![Image 29: {\displaystyle \pi _{1}X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b73939ad53b202c43c810c0d625ca2d2c10946b8) is called the [fundamental group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fundamental_group "Fundamental group") of _X_,
*   for n≥2{\displaystyle n\geq 2}![Image 30: {\displaystyle n\geq 2}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e6bf67f9d06ca3af619657f8d20ee1322da77174), π n X{\displaystyle \pi _{n}X}![Image 31: {\displaystyle \pi _{n}X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/309cf040236632f263afb0b16d69c0f0fa4f2140) are [abelian groups](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abelian_group "Abelian group") by the [Eckmann–Hilton argument](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eckmann%E2%80%93Hilton_argument "Eckmann–Hilton argument"),
*   π 0 X{\displaystyle \pi _{0}X}![Image 32: {\displaystyle \pi _{0}X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/20676791fe5303f461e0c23f2eb5c8acd8740635) can be identified with the set of path-connected components in X{\displaystyle X}![Image 33: {\displaystyle X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/68baa052181f707c662844a465bfeeb135e82bab).

Every group is the fundamental group of some space.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_theory#cite_note-2)

A map f{\displaystyle f}![Image 34: {\displaystyle f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/132e57acb643253e7810ee9702d9581f159a1c61) is called a [homotopy equivalence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_equivalence "Homotopy equivalence") if there is another map g{\displaystyle g}![Image 35: {\displaystyle g}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3556280e66fe2c0d0140df20935a6f057381d77) such that f∘g{\displaystyle f\circ g}![Image 36: {\displaystyle f\circ g}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b2f61ca7838709fbae07dce9c0d513770f10cfae) and g∘f{\displaystyle g\circ f}![Image 37: {\displaystyle g\circ f}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/10b5ad4985af48d0fb7efa3c8afa5ad7d42bfc92) are both homotopic to the identities. Two spaces are said to be homotopy equivalent if there is a homotopy equivalence between them. A homotopy equivalence class of spaces is then called a [homotopy type](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_type "Homotopy type"). There is a weaker notion: a map f:X→Y{\displaystyle f:X\to Y}![Image 38: {\displaystyle f:X\to Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/abd1e080abef4bbdab67b43819c6431e7561361c) is said to be a [weak homotopy equivalence](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Weak_homotopy_equivalence "Weak homotopy equivalence") if f∗:π n(X)→π n(Y){\displaystyle f_{*}:\pi _{n}(X)\to \pi _{n}(Y)}![Image 39: {\displaystyle f_{*}:\pi _{n}(X)\to \pi _{n}(Y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/79fa1c64342226715f9327abdcb794322fb82a7f) is an isomorphism for each n≥0{\displaystyle n\geq 0}![Image 40: {\displaystyle n\geq 0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ce8a1b7b3bc3c790054d93629fc3b08cd1da1fd0) and each choice of a base point. A homotopy equivalence is a weak homotopy equivalence but the converse need not be true.

Through the adjunction

Map⁡(X×I,Y)=Map⁡(X,Map⁡(I,Y)),h↦(x↦h(x,⋅)){\displaystyle \operatorname {Map} (X\times I,Y)=\operatorname {Map} (X,\operatorname {Map} (I,Y)),\,\,h\mapsto (x\mapsto h(x,\cdot ))}![Image 41: {\displaystyle \operatorname {Map} (X\times I,Y)=\operatorname {Map} (X,\operatorname {Map} (I,Y)),\,\,h\mapsto (x\mapsto h(x,\cdot ))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/527d6a194bf5615639a453ef75143bcba8cb2967),
a homotopy h:X×I→Y{\displaystyle h:X\times I\to Y}![Image 42: {\displaystyle h:X\times I\to Y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1f240a8efc3ead4c041585116d590cdfb0e4df75) is sometimes viewed as a map X→Y I=Map⁡(I,Y){\displaystyle X\to Y^{I}=\operatorname {Map} (I,Y)}![Image 43: {\displaystyle X\to Y^{I}=\operatorname {Map} (I,Y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4e2fa58803125782416ad8d133cb2c740c4c2a7c).

### CW complex

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Homotopy_theory&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: CW complex")]

Main article: [CW complex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CW_complex "CW complex")

A [CW complex](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CW_complex "CW complex") is a space that has a filtration X⊃⋯⊃X n⊃X n−1⊃⋯⊃X 0{\displaystyle X\supset \cdots \supset X^{n}\supset X^{n-1}\supset \cdots \supset X^{0}}![Image 44: {\displaystyle X\supset \cdots \supset X^{n}\supset X^{n-1}\supset \cdots \supset X^{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6a171084199cd91bc40152db48e752b9f3545297) whose union is X{\displaystyle X}![Image 45: {\displaystyle X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/68baa052181f707c662844a465bfeeb135e82bab) and such that

1.   X 0{\displaystyle X^{0}}![Image 46: {\displaystyle X^{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4e7ed80088727b5ba6be077bad40afbd304e84de) is a discrete space, called the set of 0-cells (vertices) in X{\displaystyle X}![Image 47: {\displaystyle X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/68baa052181f707c662844a465bfeeb135e82bab).
2.   Each X n{\displaystyle X^{n}}![Image 48: {\displaystyle X^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/268db8293666fefd75cfb00513706171948edf09) is obtained by attaching several _n_-disks, _n_-cells, to X n−1{\displaystyle X^{n-1}}![Image 49: {\displaystyle X^{n-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/98ccc911504085b636c3b947ad7534a657f18dee) via maps S n−1→X n−1{\displaystyle S^{n-1}\to X^{n-1}}![Image 50: {\displaystyle S^{n-1}\to X^{n-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ed7855b6b6419f3438d4a945794d0caf166fb7ee); i.e., the boundary of an n-disk is identified with the image of S n−1{\displaystyle S^{n-1}}![Image 51: {\displaystyle S^{n-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/742bebb03fe630674b18823a59d2c75efd0066e7) in X n−1{\displaystyle X^{n-1}}![Image 52: {\displaystyle X^{n-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/98ccc911504085b636c3b947ad7534a657f18dee).
3.   A subset U{\displaystyle U}![Image 53: {\displaystyle U}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/458a728f53b9a0274f059cd695e067c430956025) is open if and only if U∩X n{\displaystyle U\cap X^{n}}![Image 54: {\displaystyle U\cap X^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/60a7c5137e3642ff6d22d6cbc8d6720411cdec86) is open for each n{\displaystyle n}![Image 55: {\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b).

For example, a sphere S n{\displaystyle S^{n}}![Image 56: {\displaystyle S^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ee006452a59bf1eb29983b4412348b66517a2d23) has two cells: one 0-cell and one n{\displaystyle n}![Image 57: {\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b)-cell, since S n{\displaystyle S^{n}}![Image 58: {\displaystyle S^{n}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ee006452a59bf1eb29983b4412348b66517a2d23) can be obtained by collapsing the boundary S n−1{\displaystyle S^{n-1}}![Image 59: {\displaystyle S^{n-1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/742bebb03fe630674b18823a59d2c75efd0066e7) of the _n_-disk to a point. In general, every manifold has the homotopy type of a CW complex;[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_theory#cite_note-3) in fact, [Morse theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morse_theory "Morse theory") implies that a compact manifold has the homotopy type of a finite CW complex.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

Remarkably, [Whitehead's theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whitehead%27s_theorem "Whitehead's theorem") says that for CW complexes, a weak homotopy equivalence and a homotopy equivalence are the same thing.

Another important result is the approximation theorem. First, the [homotopy category](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_category "Homotopy category") of spaces is the category where an object is a space but a morphism is the homotopy class of a map. Then

**[CW approximation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CW_approximation "CW approximation")**—[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_theory#cite_note-4) There exist a functor (called the CW approximation functor)

Θ:Ho⁡(spaces)→Ho⁡(CW){\displaystyle \Theta :\operatorname {Ho} ({\textrm {spaces}})\to \operatorname {Ho} ({\textrm {CW}})}![Image 60: {\displaystyle \Theta :\operatorname {Ho} ({\textrm {spaces}})\to \operatorname {Ho} ({\textrm {CW}})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4e4e5293ea913ffcabab12a1bbde2bca66dea67c)
from the homotopy category of spaces to the homotopy category of CW complexes as well as a natural transformation

θ:i∘Θ→Id,{\displaystyle \theta :i\circ \Theta \to \operatorname {Id} ,}![Image 61: {\displaystyle \theta :i\circ \Theta \to \operatorname {Id} ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/24f2f46c7e2d87fea944783b58f2b0a5a4afe449)
where i:Ho⁡(CW)↪Ho⁡(spaces){\displaystyle i:\operatorname {Ho} ({\textrm {CW}})\hookrightarrow \operatorname {Ho} ({\textrm {spaces}})}![Image 62: {\displaystyle i:\operatorname {Ho} ({\textrm {CW}})\hookrightarrow \operatorname {Ho} ({\textrm {spaces}})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/95604963b664deaa4cb7850f63d39917b82dbd67), such that each θ X:i(Θ(X))→X{\displaystyle \theta _{X}:i(\Theta (X))\to X}![Image 63: {\displaystyle \theta _{X}:i(\Theta (X))\to X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5f9cd1839d29889d07bd58e5fa7898ad4d21862c) is a weak homotopy equivalence.

Similar statements also hold for pairs and excisive triads.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_theory#cite_note-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_theory#cite_note-6)

Explicitly, the above approximation functor can be defined as the composition of the [singular chain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_chain "Singular chain") functor S∗{\displaystyle S_{*}}![Image 64: {\displaystyle S_{*}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5d3325410f0949bfb47c30694d58411ab83c7137) followed by the geometric realization functor; see [§Simplicial set](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homotopy_theory#Simplicial_set).

The above theorem justifies a common habit of working only with CW complexes. For example, given a space X{\displaystyle X}![Image 65: {\displaystyle X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/68baa052181f707c662844a465bfeeb135e82bab), one can just define the homology of X{\displaystyle X}![Image 66: {\displaystyle X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/68baa052181f707c662844a465bfeeb135e82bab) to the homology of the CW approximation of X{\displaystyle X}![Image 67: {\displaystyle X}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/68baa052181f707c662844a465bfeeb135e82bab) (the cell structure of a CW complex

enriched_points.expanded_content: Significant developments in homotopy theory, including model categories and motivic homotopy, have reshaped its foundations, enabling new computational techniques and broader applications across mathematics.
Introduced by Leray and later refined by others, spectral sequences provide powerful tools for computing homology and cohomology, significantly influencing both topological and algebraic methods.

Homotopy theory intersects with fields like algebraic geometry, category theory, and representation theory, enhancing its theoretical framework and leading to groundbreaking results in various domains.
